cards.text: Zillah also bore Tubal-cain; he was the forger of all instruments of bronze and iron.
cards.connection: Highlights that advanced skills like engineering (seen in Aksum's stelae) are a reflection of human capacity given by God.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing the engineering of the Aksum stelae.

cards.text: She came to Jerusalem with a very great retinue, with camels bearing spices and very much gold and precious stones.
cards.connection: The visit of the Queen of Sheba connects the region to the biblical narrative and the Solomonic lineage.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing the Queen of Sheba and Ethiopian identity.

cards.text: And he rose and went. And there was an Ethiopian, a eunuch, a court official of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians...
cards.connection: Marks the first recorded African convert to Christianity and the spread of the Gospel to the region.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing the Ethiopian Eunuch.

cards.text: Envoys will come out of Egypt; Ethiopia shall quickly stretch out her hands to God.
cards.connection: A prophetic verse that became the motto of the Ethiopian Church, affirming their faith as a fulfillment of prophecy.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing the conversion of Aksum and King Ezana.

cards.text: For he is the servant of God, an avenger who carries out God's wrath on the wrongdoer.
cards.connection: Provides the theological framework for King Kaleb's 'Just War' expedition to defend Christians in Najran.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing Kaleb's invasion of Yemen.

cards.text: For I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink, I was a stranger and you welcomed me...
cards.connection: Reflected in the Christian hospitality shown by the Aksumite king during the First Hijra.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing the First Hijra.
